turns.content: I'm building a lightweight wrapper around the Result type to simplify error handling in our service layer. I've been using the std::result::Result, but I want to implement custom Deref so that when people call methods like .ok_or_else() on my wrapper, they automatically get access to the underlying Result without needing explicit unwrapping or pattern matching.

That is a great use case for implementing the Deref trait. By defining impl<T> MyWrapper<T> where T: std::ops::Deref, you can leverage automatic coercion so that any method defined on Result will be available through your wrapper. This allows your custom type to inherit behavior without duplicating logic.

So if I define a struct like MyResult<T> that wraps std::result::Result<T, E>, the Deref trait will automatically forward calls to methods on Result? What exactly does the Deref trait do in this context?

The Deref trait allows your wrapper to act as if it were the target type by returning a reference. When you implement Deref for MyResult<T> pointing to Result<T, E>, any method call on MyResult will be resolved to the corresponding method on Result through coercion. This means methods like ok_or_else() are available without extra boilerplate.

Can I also implement DerefMut if needed, and how do I ensure that my wrapper doesn't accidentally shadow existing traits? Also, what happens if the underlying type is not a Result but something else like Option?

Yes, you can also implement DerefMut to allow in-place mutation of the inner value. For types other than Result, the same pattern applies; you just need to ensure your wrapper's internal storage matches what the target trait expects. For Option<T>, for example, your MyOption would wrap std::option::Option and expose its methods via Deref.
I see. But I'm worried about performance overhead from the extra dereference layer. Is there any downside to using this pattern for high-frequency operations in our service?

There is negligible overhead because Deref simply returns a reference, which is zero-copy. The compiler will inline these calls efficiently unless you force extra bounds or use unsafe code elsewhere. For most application logic, this pattern is as fast as calling the underlying type directly.
That reassures me about performance. One last thing: can I combine this with a newtype wrapper that also implements std::fmt::Debug or other traits to make debugging easier?

Absolutely. You can implement additional traits like Debug, Display, or Error alongside Deref in your MyResult struct. This lets you customize how the wrapper appears in logs and debug output while still delegating core functionality to Result via Deref coercion.

I'm stuck on implementing a custom smart pointer where I need to delegate operations to an inner value, but my current implementation feels wrong. Here is the code I have so far: struct MyPtr<T> { inner: T } impl<T> Deref for MyPtr<T> { type Target = T; fn deref(&self) -> &T { &self.inner } } When I try to use it like a normal variable, things break down. Can you help me understand why this isn't working as expected?

The issue is that Deref only provides read-only access through the deref coercion mechanism. To allow modification of the inner value, you must also implement DerefMut which gives you a mutable reference to T. Without this trait, any attempt to mutate the wrapped content will fail because the compiler cannot automatically coerce your MyPtr into &mut T.
Okay, so I need to add DerefMut. But what exactly does that do? Is it just another reference or something special?

DerefMut is the companion trait to Deref and provides a way for types that wrap other values to be mutated directly without needing to unwrap them first. It allows you to get a &mut T from a MyPtr<T> so you can call methods on the inner value or modify it in place.
Got it, but I'm still confused about how this interacts with other traits like Clone or Debug. Do they get auto-implemented too?

Traits like Clone or Debug are not automatically implemented by Deref or DerefMut. You must explicitly implement those traits yourself if you want them to work on your wrapper type, unless you derive them before wrapping the inner value.
That makes sense. But I'm also trying to build a tool where my smart pointer should expose some internal API that normally requires ownership of the inner data. How do I handle that?

You can use Deref coercions to delegate specific methods from your wrapper type directly to the inner value, effectively exposing its public interface without needing to unwrap explicitly in every call site.
Can you show me an example where this delegation happens automatically versus when I have to do it manually?

When Deref is implemented correctly, methods like push or clear on Vec<T> will work directly on MyPtr<Vec<T>> without needing .into_inner() calls. However, if you need custom logic that isn't part of the inner type's public API, then you must implement those methods manually on your wrapper.
I see, so Deref is about transparency and delegation while DerefMut adds mutability. But what if I want to prevent accidental mutations? Should I just not implement DerefMut?

Yes, omitting DerefMut will make the wrapper immutable by default in terms of direct mutation through dereference coercion. However, users can still mutate it if they unwrap first or call methods that take ownership of inner value.
That's helpful context. One last thing - does this pattern work well with newtype wrappers too? Like when I just want to expose a single field without extra logic?
Absolutely, implementing Deref on a newtype wrapper lets you access the inner value exactly as if it were the original type. This is one of the most common use cases for this pattern in Rust.

I'm building a wrapper around an existing C library in Rust and I want to avoid writing manual dispatch for every type. Can you explain how the Deref trait lets me make my custom smart pointer automatically delegate to the inner type?

Great question. The Deref trait allows any type to behave as if it were another, so when you implement Deref for your wrapper, Rust's coercion rules will let you call methods on the wrapped value without extra boilerplate. For example, if your pointer wraps a Vec<T>, you can still use push or iter because those are available through the automatic conversion. The key is that DerefMut lets you mutate the inner value when needed, while Deref alone only reads.
That sounds convenient but I'm worried about hidden behavior. If I forget to implement DerefMut, will my wrapper still compile?

Yes, it will still compile because Deref alone satisfies the trait bounds for most read-only operations. However, any attempt to modify the inner state through your wrapper without DerefMut will fail at runtime or require you to implement a different method manually. The compiler lets you use the type as long as the necessary traits are in scope, but it does not silently hide mutation failures.
I see. But what if I want my wrapper to expose only certain methods from the inner type and hide others? Can Deref help me with that?

Deref itself doesn't filter methods; it just exposes everything from the target type. To selectively expose or restrict functionality, you typically implement a newtype wrapper or use traits like Sealed to prevent unwanted implementations. For instance, if you only want to read but never mutate, implementing Deref without DerefMut gives you exactly that behavior.
And what about using serde_json::Value as an inner type? Can I wrap it and still deserialize correctly?

Yes, you can implement Deref for your wrapper targeting serde_json::Value. Since serde_json relies on trait implementations to determine how to parse data, as long as your wrapper implements the correct traits like Serialize and Deserialize, the deserialization will work seamlessly through the Deref coercion.
That makes sense. But what if I have a custom allocator that tracks memory usage? Can I use Deref to get access to that?

If your smart pointer wraps a Box or Vec and you want to inspect its allocation metadata, you can implement Deref to expose the underlying type. However, some allocator-specific fields may not be public by default, so you might need to adjust visibility or use traits like Debug or IntoIterator to safely access them.
So the real power here is that I don't have to re-implement every method from scratch. Is there a pattern where I implement Deref and then just copy-paste some inner methods?

Exactly. You implement Deref to delegate, then you can safely reuse the inner type's logic for most operations. The only parts you need to write are your own wrapper-specific behavior, like custom allocation hooks or additional validation. This pattern saves significant boilerplate while keeping your API clean and ergonomic.
